sentences.text: My name is Helen Mark-- M-A-R-K.
My birthday is July 2, 1919.
I was born in Vienna, Austria.
Well, I was the only child.
And my parents-- well, my father was Viennese.
My mother was Czech-- Czechoslovakian.
My father was Robert Bellak-- B-E-L-L-A-K.
And my mother was Ani.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I grew up in Vienna as the only child.
But my parents got divorced when I was 8 years old, and I stayed with my mother and lived with my mother.
I went to school in Vienna for eight years, in one school, and finished high school there.
And then I had two years of commercial school.
And then I left Vienna with my mother after Hitler came, and went and moved to Czechoslovakia.
And then I lived in Prague.
I'll-- yeah, I grew up in a normal apartment house in Vienna, which wasn't very, very high.
There were six stories only.
And we had the fourth floor.
And it was a fairly large, nice apartment.
And as a child, I had a governess who came to me when I was born, and stayed for eight years.
And then I had a very nice young Swiss lady to learn French.
And she stayed with me for year and a 1/2.
And then I had an English lady to learn English.
And she stayed with me, usually, from the time I came from school in the afternoon-- 2 o'clock or so-- till evening.
They didn't live in.
And then I was sent to England once, in summer, to learn English.
And stayed there for three months.
That was great.
It was-- first of all, the first time out of the country alone without my parents or my governess.
I went with a group of young ladies who went to-- in exchange for Austrian girls coming to Austria.
And we had a very, very lovely group-- and had to take along our Austrian so-called national dresses that were the-- what they called dirndl-- with the white blouse and the aprons.
And those were worn in all of Austria in the country.
And we were told to take them along, because we were going to Sandringham Palace to be introduced to the queen and king of that time.
And we were dressed in those dresses.
And that was a tremendous, exciting time for all of us.
We were 17 years old.
Yeah, uh-huh-- the old--
--the old Queen Mary-- the great-grandmother of Elizabeth.
And that was in Sandringham Palace, which is up north in England.
And it was very, very exciting for us, of course.
And so it was a very great time.
And we had a, you know, a lot of fun together, too, because, you know, we were away from home first time.
And that was very nice.
And then I went back to Vienna, of course.
And the last year when Hitler came, I couldn't go to school anymore.
My best friend is a young-- well, not so young lady anymore-- who now lives in America.
And we went to school from first grade on.
Her name is Herta Sperry, S-P-E-R-R-Y.
And I still meet her quite often.
And I visit her in New Jersey, where they live.
And we meet every year in Vienna, which we both visit, and have some friends there still, and all get together.
And then I had another very good friend whose mother was a very close friend of my mother.
So we grew up together.
And she lives in London, England.
And they had all left in 1938, which-- under Hitler then.
And--
And my father and I met twice a week in the afternoon.
We went and had a cup of cocoa and cake, or whatever.

And then when I grew up and was a little older, I went a lot to the opera or concerts with him, because he was very, very fond of music and wanted me to grow up getting acquainted with music and getting to like it, and love it like he did.
So I spent some very beautiful times at the Vienna Opera and the concert hall.
And aside of that, I love sport.
I did ice-skating.
I did speed skating.
I did figure skating.
And I played-- I skied, of course, because I was very close to Vienna.
You didn't have to go far in order to get on a little hill to ski.
And then I played tennis in summer and did a little swimming for a club.
So I was really into mostly enjoying sports.
No.
I wasn't very-- [LAUGHS] very great in school, I must admit.
For instance, I took speed skating, and my mother was very much against it.
And so I told her that I am going to go very early in the morning and learned typewriting in school.
But I went to the ice rink and had training for speed skating, till they found out about it when I didn't have any credits for my typewriting in the-- in the-- at the end of the year.
So I was more interested in that than-- than in school.
And I wasn't very-- very good.
And I don't think I ever would have gone on to study, go to college, or so.
It wasn't-- it wasn't me.
Do you remember what you were dreaming of becoming when you grew up?
Where?
No.
My only dream-- no, I didn't really.
I-- studies-- certainly not.
The only thing that I wanted to achieve was in sport.
And that I did, to a certain degree, till 1938 came.
And that put an end to it.
My grandparents died when I was very young.
They were from Czechoslovakia my-- on my mother's side.
And they had come from Czechoslovakia to Austria in-- oh, I don't know when it was.
But my mother, I think, was 17 years old when she moved to Vienna.
And my grandparents, on my father's side-- my grandfather had coal mines in Austria, and in Italy, and in Hungary.
And my father didn't want to go into that, so he went into the stock exchange business.
But both my grandmothers and my grandfathers died when I was, oh, between 7 and 10 years old.
So I really didn't have much chance to get to, you know, to remember too much.
I know I had to go every Sunday for lunch to my mother's parents.
And as a child, that was kind of boring-- all the grownups.
And you were not allowed to talk unless you are talked to.
And it was kind of strict, so I didn't like that too much.
But my mother, I saw very little of, really, because I was in school.
And then I had the so-called governess or whatever they were called later on when I was little older.
And I saw her sometimes in the evening.
We had dinner together.
But very often, she was out.
So I grew up more with-- with these ladies who were taking care of me and going out with me, if I had to do things, you know.
And I was a little bit scared of my mother.
She was very strict.
And I had adored my father and was very hurt when he left.
And, you know, being an 8-year-old-- not quite 18-- 8-year-old child-- I blamed her for leaving me, because that's all I understood at that time-- that I lost him.
And so my mother and I only got close later on when I was more grown up.
And then we became friends when I got to know her.
But then she got to know me better.
And then we moved to Czechoslovakia.
Vienna was a very fun-loving city and a very beautiful city.
And there were lots of-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --opportunity to go to dancing.
And, you know, everybody was very much living it up.

And there was just a lot of-- around the city-- a lot of places which are called the so-called new wine places-- where you go in the evening, and you sit in the garden, and you drink the new wine, and eat some food that you either brought along or got there.
And there was music.
And that was the thing that people enjoyed very much.
The theater was great.

And Vienna is a very, very beautiful city with a lot of parks and-- in the city, in the center, and lots of old palaces of the emperor's time; marvelous food-- much too good-- with a lot of whipped cream.
And it was a very fun time.
And it was kind of, you know, it was like all the good times before the end.
Everybody was already talking about what's going on in Germany, and believing that it could never happen to us, and it never would come to Austria.
And the Austrians, of course, you know, wouldn't do it, which was wrong.
We were all living under illusions.
German, because my father spoke no Czech.
So at home we spoke German.
And later on, I spoke a little-- when I moved, I spoke more Czech.
But at home, in Vienna, I spoke German most of the time, except with, as I say, with my so-called lady companions-- either French or English.
But in-- at-- at home, with my parents, I spoke German.
I grew up--
Because after Hitler had taken Austria, they closed the border between Czechoslovakia and Austria, as far as money was concerned.
You could no longer send-- the Czechs didn't allow it-- to send money from Prague or from Czechoslovakia to Austria.
And my mother's-- had most of her money in Czechoslovakia.
So the time came when she wanted to move to Prague again.
And her sister, who lived in Vienna, went along, also.
She was not married.
And they thought, again, you know, Czechoslovakia-- Hitler's not going to come to Czechoslovakia, which was [LAUGHS] exactly one year later, he arrived.
But everybody always thought, you know, it can't happen here.
And it can't happen to us.
So we moved.
And I had a visa to go to England.
And my mother couldn't get out after Hitler arrived, because one of her sisters was just-- her family had left the country the same night illegally.
And so my mother and her other sister were kind of taken hostage.
They couldn't get an exit visa.
And I did, because I was of age.
And I had a visa to England.
I had my airline ticket.
My luggage was gone.
And my mother cried so terribly that I didn't have the heart to leave her.
And I stayed.

And then a cousin of mine, who lived in Czechoslovakia, in a small town where there were textile factories that he owned-- and he was planning on going to either America or Canada, and told me if I want to come and learn in the factory, he'll take me along.
So I left Prague and went with him, and stayed three months in the factory there.
And then it was too close to the Austrian border, that little town, and it got dangerous.
So I went back to Prague.
And he went on with his family to Canada, and had a visa for me, again, to go with them.

And again, I stayed, because I didn't want to leave my mother; which today I'm glad I didn't, because I stayed with her till-- as long as she was alive-- and made it definitely easier for her, and could help a little bit.
And in Prague, I made a very, very dear friend, again, with a girl who unfortunately did not survive, but had a younger sister who had gone to England.
And I never met her in Prague.
But after the war, I met her here in California.
And it was like a continuation of friendship that I had with her older sister.
So she is my closest friend now.
But I spent a lot of time in her parents' house in Prague.
And we had an 8 o'clock curfew.
We were not allowed to go out of the house from 8:00 in the evening till 8:00 in the morning.

So the few young people who had, you know, been left in Prague, we always went to my girlfriend's house, because they had the largest room where we could all kind of lie down on the floor and stay overnight.
And so we all became very, very close-- but especially Margot.

And then all of a sudden, they asked for-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --four-- well, in my case, in one little village in Czechoslovakia, they had asked for two men and two women to come and do labor on the field.
That was 1940.
So I went with one young man, and my girlfriend went with another young man to that little town.
And we worked in the fields and in the house, you know, taking care of animals, and of cows, and so on; and were there for two months, and did labor for-- for the farmers.
And then they sent us back to Prague again.
And then shortly after that, they started with transport-- 1941.
And so I stayed in Prague till December '41.
Then I went to Terezin.
Well, they sent us a postcard to come on a certain day with 30 kilo of luggage, including bedding, food, clothing, everything.
And come to a-- it's a big hall of-- exhibition hall, I would-- I think it's called.
And so we went there-- my mother, her sister-- who was still with us-- and me.
And we stayed there for about three days and were registered.
And, you know, we had to give them the key to our apartment, which we had left with everything in it.
And it took a few days to get us-- three days, I think-- to get us ready.
And something happened there, which had a great influence on my life in Terezin, because I met a-- I was asked to help out at the doctor's tent.
He had a tent there, and if somebody was really ill, he had to examine them.
And then they were sent home or not, depending on what he found.
And I was supposed to stand guard in front of the door to that tent.
And I had a chair.
And one lady came with her very old mother, who didn't feel good.
And I gave her my chair and had her sit down.

And later on, this same lady, the daughter, when we came to Terezin, helped me to get a position which made life a little easier for me from the beginning, because she wanted to kind of say thank you.
But anyway, after three days, we got on a train-- regular train-- and went to Bohusovice, because there was no railway going into Terezin yet.
And then we marched from there into the ghetto.
And that was a little town, which was originally built by Empress Maria Theresa, for the army.
And that's why the town has mostly army barracks-- huge, huge army barracks-- which had been closed for, I don't know, 50 or 60 years, or how long-- or even longer, probably.
And what they did first-- it was a Czech village.
And we were only in army barracks.
And we were not allowed to go out.
And every time a new transport came, they opened another barrack.
They had women and men separate.
And children, depending on the age, were either-- the little boys with their father, and little girls went with their mother.
And each army barrack had their own kitchen.
And so the food was brought there and cooked there.
And we were in there without going out, except if you had a separate permission, because you had some kind of a job that demanded that you walk around in the town from one barrack to the other.
Then it was easy, and you could get out and walk around during the day, at least.
And that's what this lady did for me, whose mother I had helped.
She became the, what they called barrack's eldest.
Everybody who was in charge of something was always the eldest.
Like in Terezin, the head of the Jewish community-- of the Jewish people-- who-- who ran the ghetto-- was a man of 35 years old.
But he was called Jewish eldest.
Well, all that-- that happened when we arrived, really, that we were brought into the army barracks.
And there were some small rooms and some big ones.
And some were 110 people, and some were only 20.
And some were-- I was very lucky.
I got one for my mother, my aunt, and two of their friends, which was a very small room.
And I stayed in the next room, which was connected with a few other girls, that we were 12 in one room.
And we really had nothing to do.
We-- we couldn't go out.
I-- after a little while, I did.
But most of the women, like my mother, was for months in there without ever getting out on the street.
And there were-- after a little while, we had to work.
But at the beginning, there was nothing-- no work, no-- just sit and wait for the day to go by.
I was-- in '41, I was 22, yeah.
My mother was-- I think, 48-- 48 or 49.
She died in '44 and was 50 years old.
So yeah, she was 47.
And then I started to work in an office, which was in charge of putting people to work.
Some people were-- in that office were running the men's division and some the women.
And I worked for the head of the woman's division.
And that was because we had to supply everything that a little town needed.
Because they took all the Czech people out of the-- of that town and gave us all the little houses.
Because there were more and more people coming-- more transports.
So we had to do our own bread baking, and we had to do our own laundry.
And we had to do the big-- I mean, the you know, big-- not for one person, but for a whole-- barracks or so.
And all the labor had to be done by us.
And we had to see that-- to put everybody to work.
And then there was a lot of labor demanded by the Germans.
We had to make uniforms.
We had to build things.
There was a big place for-- where they-- I don't know, really, what they made.
But it was only out of wood-- whatever they made.
They made caskets for them, and they made all kind of-- and these people had to be selected and trained a little bit, and then sent to work for either the Germans or for running of the-- of Terezin.
Every kitchen had to feed about 20,000 people.
So we had to do-- get the cooks.
We had to get the people who transported materials within the little town.
So everybody had to somehow get to work.
And one day, we got a order to send 1,000 women into an area in Czechoslovakia to plant trees-- a wood-- woods.
And I was in charge of this.
And so I also put the group together for myself with all my friends.
And we were taken by train to the little town, which I had known since I was a small child.
And that's why I wanted to go, because my family had some textile factories there.
And I had gone on vacation in summer very often there.
And I know-- knew the kids I played with.
And I knew a lot of the workers.
And so I figured, if I go, I might get some-- somebody who I know who would-- who I could trust.
And sure enough, at the railroad station, there was an old man who worked for the family all his life.
And he knew me since I was riding on his shoulder as a little girl.
And he was very smart.
He came.
And they had trucks they had to put our knapsacks and bedding, which we had to take along on the truck and drive it up on the mountain.
So he came to me, asked me what number group I have, and that was it.
And by the time we came to work, we were only guarded by Czech forester, and only had Czech gendarmes in the little house that we lived in.
We were 12 in my group.
So it was much, much easier.
And sure enough, we got there.
And after two days, somebody came and-- hiding in the wood-- and motioned me.
And I went there, and he said, look in the trees.
And they had put at night all kind of packages up in the trees with food and stuff.
So we got that.

And then one of the gendarmes whom we befriended-- and he was willing to go to Prague and the other town, to Brunn, which also had girls with us in that group-- and he went and brought us cigarettes, which was more than gold in Terezin.
You could get a lot more for that than-- money didn't mean anything.
And he brought us food.
And he brought all kind of news, and so on.
So that was marvelous for us.
And we were really very lucky, because the Czech gendarmes were totally on our side.
And of course, they had to be very careful.
But still, they helped us.
And the Czech forester was a nice man.
And we just worked during the day.
And we cooked at night.
We had one of those-- we lived in a-- in a former little restaurant with two rooms.
And each had a coal stove.
And we got coal.
And we cooked, and we baked.
And we ate a lot.
And then when we went back to Terezin after two months, I think we looked too well.
The SS man who was the-- the head of the SS in Terezin at the station, yelled and screamed-- that those fat Jewish sows-- as he called us-- look at them.
[LAUGHS] So-- we looked too good.
But--
